title: Tokenization Basics
concept: Tokenization splits a continuous string of text into smaller units (tokens), such as words or characters, serving as the first step in the NLP pipeline.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to split text into tokens?

options.text: It prepares raw text for NLP models to understand.

exercises.explain: Just like a recipe needs ingredients chopped up before cooking, Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms need raw text chopped into tokens before they can analyze it.

cards.title: Can Computers Read?
cards.body: When you read this sentence, your brain instantly splits the letters into separate words. But to a computer, a sentence is just one giant, continuous wall of text. How do computers make sense of where words start and end? They use a trick called tokenization!

cards.title: What is Tokenization?
cards.body: Tokenization is the process of cutting a long block of text into smaller, bite-sized pieces called 'tokens'. These tokens can be whole words, parts of words, or even single characters. It is the very first step in helping computers read human language.

cards.title: The Puzzle Pieces
cards.body: Imagine a puzzle. Before you can build it, you have to break the big picture down into individual puzzle pieces. Tokenization is just like breaking that picture apart so your computer can inspect and understand each piece one by one!

cards.title: Tokenizing a Sentence
cards.body:
If we feed the sentence "I love coding" into a word tokenizer, it chops it up into three separate pieces:
1. "I"
2. "love"
3. "coding"
Now, a machine learning model can easily count, analyze, and process each word!

cards.title: Myth: Tokens are only words
cards.body: Many people think tokens are always whole, dictionary words. Actually, tokens can be single characters, punctuation marks (like exclamation points), or even parts of words (like splitting "unhelpful" into "un" and "helpful").

cards.title: Words vs. Characters
cards.body: Depending on the task, you can split text differently. Word tokenization keeps words whole, while character tokenization breaks everything down to individual letters.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of tokenization?

options.text: Splitting text into smaller pieces so a computer can process it
Translating text directly from English to Spanish
Correcting spelling mistakes in a paragraph
Encrypting passwords to keep them secure

exercises.explain: Tokenization is the essential first step in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that breaks raw, continuous text down into manageable pieces so algorithms can analyze them.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A tokenizer can treat punctuation marks (like "." or "?") as separate tokens.
exercises.explain: Punctuation marks (like commas, periods, and question marks) are very important for meaning, so tokenizers treat them as separate tokens.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to explain the result of tokenization.
exercises.sentence: Tokenization splits a continuous string of text into smaller units called [blank].
exercises.explain: Tokenization cuts down a continuous string of text into smaller pieces called tokens, which are usually words, subwords, or characters.

exercises.prompt: When you send a message to an AI assistant, how does it process your input?

options.text: The AI reads the entire text block at once without breaking it down.
The AI breaks the message down into tokens first before generating a reply.
The AI translates the message into a different human language first.

exercises.explain: AI chatbots do not read entire paragraphs as one giant block. They must first tokenize the text to read and process it word-by-word or character-by-character.

exercises.prompt: You want to split the sentence "Let's learn!" into word tokens. Which of the following shows a correct word-level tokenization?
exercises.explain: To tokenize the sentence "Let's learn!" into words, the computer splits it at spaces and isolates punctuation: "Let's", "learn", and "!". "Let" by itself is not how the word is written in the original sentence, and keeping it all as one single block defeats the purpose of splitting!
